one_line_takeaway: Injecting Gaussian noise into a network's hidden activations at inference time drives predictions from sample-dependent to a model-specific stationary distribution, which cheaply measures sample regularity under small noise and exposes backdoor target-class collapse under large noise.
key_finding: In the large-noise regime, benign models converge to a dispersed stationary output distribution whereas backdoored models collapse so that the vast majority of Monte Carlo runs predict the backdoor target class, giving backdoored models normalized output entropy near zero versus substantially higher entropy for benign models (Fig 10, across five attacks and three architecture/dataset pairs). Non-optimized input-level perturbation does NOT reproduce this target-class concentration (Fig 9, ten CIFAR-100 backdoored ResNet-18 models), making the collapse a distinctive activation-space signature. In the small-noise regime, escape noise decreases monotonically as the random-label fraction rises from 0 to 80% (CIFAR-10 ResNet-18, Fig 4) and correlates strongly with both memorization and consistency scores on ImageNet and CIFAR-100 (ResNet-50, Fig 5), while requiring only a single trained model rather than the shadow-model ensembles that memorization scores need. Deeper CNNs show stronger target collapse; vision transformers (DeiT) show only partial, attenuated collapse.
core_contribution: The paper introduces APEX (Activation Perturbation for EXploration), a general inference-time probing paradigm that injects i.i.d. Gaussian noise into a trained network's hidden post-activations after every layer while keeping the input x and parameters theta fixed, then estimates the output-class distribution over T stochastic forward passes. Its central theoretical claim (Theorem 3.1) is a decomposition of the perturbed final activation into a noise-driven, input-independent term sigma*v_L plus a bounded, input-dependent residual r_L(x;sigma); as sigma grows the input-dependent logit term e(x;sigma)/sigma vanishes, so predictions transition from sample-dependent to a model-specific stationary distribution. The paper also frames ordinary input perturbation as a constrained special case of activation perturbation (perturbations confined to the image of the prefix-network Jacobian). This general paradigm is the core contribution; backdoor detection is one of two downstream case studies, the other being a non-security sample-regularity/memorization probe.
threat_model: Defense/analysis oriented, and deliberately scoped by the paper itself. Backdoors are used as a testbed (Section 5.2) because the induced bias is 'semantically interpretable' and known to live in internal representations, not proposed as a deployed, hardened detector. The setting assumes white-box access to intermediate activations of a suspect trained model (to inject per-layer noise and run repeated forward passes); no access to the trigger, poisoned data, or a clean reference model is required. The observation is that a backdoored model's large-noise stationary output distribution concentrates on the attacker's target class while a benign model's stays dispersed, letting a defender flag the presence of a backdoor and read off the likely target label. The paper notes attenuated/partial collapse for transformer architectures, so the signal is not universal. It does not claim to remove backdoors or to be robust against adaptive attackers designing around the stationary-distribution signature.
limitations: Evidence is overwhelmingly qualitative and figure-based; the main text reports almost no precise scalar metrics (no ROC/AUC or detection accuracy for backdoor detection, no numeric correlation coefficients), so claims of 'strong correlation' and 'collapse' rest on visual trends. The backdoor-detection use is a testbed observation, not a validated or adaptive-attacker-tested detector, and requires white-box activation access. The target-collapse signal weakens on vision transformers (DeiT re-disperse at larger noise), limiting architecture generality. Theorem 3.1's constructive constants and the stationary-distribution convergence proof are deferred to appendices. Escape noise as a regularity metric is validated against, not shown to outperform, existing memorization/consistency scores; its advantage is claimed cost (single model, Monte Carlo inference) rather than accuracy.

quantitative_results: Average escape noise on CIFAR-10 ResNet-18 decreases monotonically as the random-label ratio increases from 0% to 80% (Fig 4)

Escape noise (smallest sigma at which predicted-class probability drops below 0.5) correlates strongly with memorization score and consistency C-score on ImageNet and CIFAR-100 using ResNet-50 (Fig 5); correlations reported graphically, no coefficient given

Under large activation noise, backdoored models show normalized output entropy near zero (highly concentrated on target class) vs substantially higher, dispersed entropy for benign models, across 5 attacks and 3 architecture/dataset pairs (Fig 10)
On ten CIFAR-100 backdoored ResNet-18 models, activation perturbation drives average predicted probability of the backdoor target class toward ~1 while input perturbation does not (Fig 9)
Normalized stationary-distribution entropy averaged over ten Inception models on CIFAR-100 is higher (more dispersed) for activation perturbation than for input perturbation (Fig 7)
Deeper CNNs (X+2Net, 6-9 convolutional layers) under BadNets show progressively stronger target-class concentration / lower entropy (Fig 11)
Gaussian, uniform, and Laplace noise yield nearly identical stationary output distributions (Appendix O); results largely qualitative/figure-based rather than tabulated scalar metrics
baselines_compared: Memorization score (Feldman; Feldman & Zhang) as a sample-regularity reference metric
Consistency / C-score (Jiang et al.) as a sample-regularity reference metric
